coredata.dc:title: Facile preparation of surface functional carbon dots and their application in doxorubicin hydrochloride delivery
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Xin
Wang, Yongbo
Liu, Wenwei
Liang, Xuhua
Si, Boni
Liu, Enzhou
Hu, Xiaoyun
Fan, Jun
coredata.dc:description: A novel form of carbon dots (PEG-CDs) was prepared by using one-step hydrothermal synthesis with glycine as the precursor and polyethylene glycol (PEG)-2000 as the surface modifier. The prepared PEG-CDs were characterized with HRTEM, XRD, PL, FTIR and UV. The results showed that they were quasi spherical particles with a diameter of approximately 12nm and had a fine fluorescence property, good water solubility, favorable stability and high quantum yield with 28.58%. Doxorubicin hydrochloride (DOX) as a potential anti-cancer drug was loaded on the PEG-CDs. The drug loading capacity was 24.25mg/g and the encapsulation efficiency was 77.23% at pH 9.0 with 36h of dialysis; DOX release in PBS system with a pH of 5.5 could reach up to 76.67% in 23h.
coredata.dcterms:subject: PEG-CDs
Functional
Luminescence
DOX
Drug loading
Drug release

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The factors of affecting PEG-CDs luminescent, PEG-2000 dosage (a), temperature (b), time (c), the fluorescence intensity and ultraviolet absorption of PEG-CDs on optimal condition (d).
TEM image (a), particle distribution (b), XRD pattern (c) and FTIR spectra (d) (a, b, c, d was PEG-CDs, CDs, pure glycine and PEG 2000, respectively).
DOX loaded on PEG-CDs (a) and DOX releasing (b).
The process of PEG-CDs loading DOX.
The specific FTIR information of samples.

head.ce:title: Facile preparation of surface functional carbon dots and their application in doxorubicin hydrochloride delivery

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering, Northwest University, No. 229 Taibai North Road, Xi’an, Shaanxi 710069, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmacy, ShaanXi University of Chinese Medicine, Xianyang, Shaanxi 712046, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Physics, Northwest University, No. 229 Taibai North Road, Xi’an, Shaanxi 710069, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel form of carbon dots (PEG-CDs) was prepared by using one-step hydrothermal synthesis with glycine as the precursor and polyethylene glycol (PEG)-2000 as the surface modifier. The prepared PEG-CDs were characterized with HRTEM, XRD, PL, FTIR and UV. The results showed that they were quasi spherical particles with a diameter of approximately 12nm and had a fine fluorescence property, good water solubility, favorable stability and high quantum yield with 28.58%. Doxorubicin hydrochloride (DOX) as a potential anti-cancer drug was loaded on the PEG-CDs. The drug loading capacity was 24.25mg/g and the encapsulation efficiency was 77.23% at pH 9.0 with 36h of dialysis; DOX release in PBS system with a pH of 5.5 could reach up to 76.67% in 23h.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) are novel fluorescent materials and have been paid great attention by researchers in recent years. CDs are nano crystalline with a quasi spherical structure and their luminescence performance depend on particle dimensions and excitation wavelength; besides they have stable fluorescence property and anti-bleaching nature [1]. Compared to traditional semiconductor quantum dots [2,3], CDs have notable virtues, such as low toxicity, favorable biocompatibility, convenient synthesis on a large-scale and low cost [4,5]. The researches on CDs are mainly focused on biological imaging and molecular detection, however, they are rarely applied to drug delivery systems. Though there are many methods for CDs preparation, some problems such that their morphology and dimension are difficult to be controlled due to a variety carbon precursors, which restrict their applications. Moreover nano medicine supporters are important for studying drug carrier systems, in which CdS quantum dots, carbon nano tubes and graphene as carriers were used to load a given drug [2,6]. But there has been little attention applied to CDs as an efficient drug carrier system and reports on their drug loading and release mechanism.

Doxorubicin hydrochloride (DOX) is an antineoplastic antibiotic, entering tumor cells via the cytomembrane with superior pharmacological activity that can restrain composing RNA and DNA, and is an inhibitor of reverse transcriptase and RNA polymerase, applied to treating breast cancer, thyroid carcinoma, lung cancer and oophoroma [7]. The amino and carboxyl in glycine can initiate condensation reactions and form an amido bond that is in favor of generating CDs [8]. In this paper PEG-CDs were prepared facilely with glycine as carbon precursor and PEG-2000 as modifier by the one step hydrothermal method; DOX as an anti-cancer drug were used to be loaded on PEG-CDs. The drug release from DOX-PEG-CDs system were also studied.

All chemicals were analytical grade reagents that had not been further purified. The PEG-CDs were prepared as follows: moderate amount of glycine and PEG-2000 were dissolved in deionized water, mixed and then placed in high pressure reactor with Teflon liner and carried on hydrothermal reaction. The produced PEG-CDs were obtained by filtering the mixture with 0.22μm microporous membranes. The factors affecting the fluorescence property of PEG-CDs, including modifier dosage, hydrothermal temperature, time and initial pH of mixture were investigated with the purpose of optimizing PEG-CDs preparation. Quinine sulfate (Ys
=0.540) was used to determine the quantum yield of PEG-CDs calculated with (1):
(1)
Y
u
= (
F
u
/
A
u
) (
A
s
/
F
s
)
Y
s
where Yu , Ys , Fu , Fs , Au and As are the quantum yield, fluorescence integral intensity, and UV absorbance of determinand and reference compound, respectively [9].

ce:section.ce:section-title: The experiments of PEG-CDs loading DOX and drug release
ce:section.ce:para:
5mL 0.125mg/mL of DOX and 10mL 2.0mg/mL of PEG-CDs were mixed and stirred slowly in the dark for 12h. Then they were transferred to a dialysis bag with 200mL of deionized water outside. DOX outside dialysis bag was analyzed with UV spectrometry at 480nm [7]. The loading amount and encapsulation efficiency of DOX were calculated with the following formulae [10]:
(2)
Loading
amount = (
m
0
-
m
1
) /
m
CQDs
(3)
Encapsulation
efficiency = (
m
0
-
m
1
) /
m
0
× 100 %
where
m
0
is the total amount of DOX, and
m
1
is the amount of DOX without loaded. DOX release from the drug loading system were achieved with static dialysis. Phosphate buffer solutions (PBS) with pH 7.4 and 5.5 were used to study DOX release in situ. 2mL of solution outside dialysis bag were taken out to be analyzed their UV absorbance at intervals, then 2mL of PBS with corresponding pH as a supplement was added to the external solution. The cumulative release efficiency of DOX was determined according to (4):
(4)
Cumulative
release
efficiency = [
C
i
·
V
0
+ (
C
1
+
C
2
+
C
3
+ … … +
C
i - 1
) · V ] /
m
0
× 100 %
where Ci is the concentration of the i-th sample;
V
0
and V are the volumes of release media and sample extracted at a time [11], respectively;
m
0
is the total drug amount of the release system.

The fluorescence and UV–vis absorption spectra of PEG-CDs prepared on different conditions including the dosage of modifier, hydrothermal temperature and time are displayed in Fig. 1 . The optimal conditions of PEG-CDs preparation should be 1:2 mass ratio for glycine and PEG-2000, 180°C and 10h of reaction time based on the Fig. 1a–c. Initial pH value of the mixture adjusted from 4 to 10 had not distinct effects on the luminescence of PEG-CDs; therefore, their corresponding spectra are not shown. The UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra for PEG-CDs prepared at optimal conditions were showed in Fig. 1d. The results suggested that the maximum excitation and emission wavelength of PEG-CDs were at 365nm and 435nm respectively. The quantum yield of PEG-CDs was 28.58%.

The preparation of two CDs were displayed in Scheme 1 . PEG-CDs appeared nattier blue fluorescence at 365nm and their luminescence intensity was obviously stronger than CDs without PEG-2000. Therefore, PEG-2000 as a functional modifier could improve fluorescence properties of PEG-CDs and their stability.

Fig. 2 a is the TEM image of PEG-CDs and indicated that they had a quasi-spherical structure with well dispersity. The particle dimension of PEG-CDs changed from 5nm to 21nm and had a mean value of 12nm in Fig. 2b. The particle size of PEG-CDs were larger than that of reported CDs, due to the addition of PEG-2000 which could enrich the surface groups of CDs and increased their dimensions [12]. The XRD pattern of PEG-CDs had a diffraction peak at 2 theta of 27° in Fig. 2c, which suggested that PEG-CDs possessed graphite phase structure with a highly disordered carbon atom [13]. Fig. 2d is the FTIR spectra of samples, where the absorption peaks at signed “1” and “8” in line “a” were strengthened by adding PEG-2000, corresponding to methyne and the secondary alcohol CO bond derived from PEG-2000 respectively [14], which demonstrated the surface of PEG-CDs were functionalized by PEG-2000 mainly. The other specific IR information are listed in Table 1 .

The effect of pH on the drug loading amount and encapsulation efficiency of DOX are shown in Fig. 3 a. DOX was loaded weakly under acidic conditions and especially this loading performance was poor at pH of 5.0. Nevertheless the capability of DOX loaded was improved with increasing pH and the loading drug amount and encapsulation percentage of DOX were to 24.25mg/g and 77.23% at pH 9.0, which was better than that of 13.53mg/g and 43.08% at pH 7.2. It could be seen that DOX loaded on PEG-CDs was effected by pH, the reason may be that NH2 of DOX were protonated in the acidic medium, which increased their solubility and restrained their loading on PEG-CDs [14]. Three loading drug modes in PEG-CDs-DOX system are hydrogen bonds that H atom of PEG-CDs link O and N of DOX as covalent bond [7], electrostatic interaction between COOH of PEG-CDs and NH2 of DOX [4], and π-π stacking consisting in big π bond of benzenes in PEG-CDs and in DOX [7]. The process of DOX loaded on PEG-CDs might be imitated as shown in Scheme 2 .

However, the destination of the DOX-PEG-CDs system was to reach nidi and fulfill drug release. The extracellular microenvironment of tumor cells were simulated by PBS with pH 7.4 and 5.5, and hence the release performance of DOX from the PEG-CDs drug delivery system was investigated. In Fig. 3b there was a distinct release change from the 5-th to 9-th h for the PBS system at pH 5.5. Subsequently, DOX release increased slowly to 76.67%. The release process of DOX was slower in the PBS system at pH 7.4 with 59.98% of release percentage in 23h. So the release of DOX loaded on PEG-CDs was superior in the weak acid environment. As was shown in Scheme 2, DOX were loaded on the PEG-CDs mainly by depending on hydrogen bond and electrostatic interaction, thus they could dissociate easily from the surface of PEG-CDs on acidic conditions, and further DOX have a higher solubility on acidic condition due to the increased protonation of NH2 groups on DOX [7]. Therefore, this DOX-PEG-CDs system had a high releasing efficiency in the PBS with pH 5.5 which is accordance with pH of the extracellular microenvironment of tumor cells primarily, and consequently will be good potential clinical application in drug delivery.

In summary, glycine as carbon source was used to prepare CDs functionalized with PEG-2000. These PEG-CDs were characterized with HRTEM, XRD, PL, FTIR and UV, indicating they had uniform particle size of approximately 12nm, high fluorescence intensity, good stability and 28.58% of quantum yield. When DOX were loaded on the PEG-CDs, the loading drug capacity with 24.25mg/g and encapsulation efficiency with 77.23% for DOX were achieved on the condition of pH at 9.0 and 36h of dialysis, and DOX release efficiency could reach up to 76.67% in PBS system with pH 5.5 in 23h. Therefore, the PEG-CDs are promising carbon nano-materials for drug delivery.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (grants: 51372201, 21476183, and 21676213).
